items.title: Sprint index bounds validation gap
items.description: The sprint loop trusts resolveSprintRange() to return valid bounds, but there's no redundant check immediately before the array access ep.Sprints[sprintNum-1]. If resolveSprintRange() has a logic error or sprints are mutated between validation and access, this panics.
items.fix: Add a defensive bounds check immediately before the array access, or add an assertion in resolveSprintRange() that guarantees endSprint <= len(sprints).

items.title: Add verification runner edge case tests
items.description: Current tests cover standard check types well but miss: command timeout behavior (120s limit), large output truncation (10MB cap), regex pattern edge cases (special characters, empty patterns), and interaction between check types.
items.fix: Add test cases for timeout expiry, output exceeding 10MB, pathological regex patterns, and multi-check verification runs with mixed pass/fail.

items.title: Structured JSON build reports
items.description: All build output is currently unstructured terminal text. A --json-report flag would emit a build-report.json containing sprint results, verification outcomes, heal attempt counts, audit findings, and timing data. The data already exists scattered across .fry/ files.
items.fix: Create internal/report/report.go with BuildReport struct. Define nested types: SprintResult, VerificationResult, HealSummary, AuditSummary. Aggregate data at the end of executeRun() in internal/cli/run.go. Marshal to JSON and write to build-report.json. Add --json-report flag to runCmd. Add build-report.json to .gitignore. Update docs/commands.md. Add tests.

items.title: @check_test verification primitive
items.description: Currently users must use @check_cmd with raw shell commands to run tests. A dedicated @check_test primitive would understand test frameworks (Go, npm/jest, pytest), parse pass/fail/skip counts, and report structured results.
items.fix: Add CheckTest to the check type enum in internal/verify/types.go. Add parsing for @check_test in internal/verify/parser.go. Add execution logic in internal/verify/runner.go — run command, parse output for test counts. Detect framework from command prefix. Update docs/verification.md and templates/verification-example.md. Add tests.

items.title: Heuristic-only --continue mode (no LLM cost)
items.description: The current --continue mode invokes an LLM to analyze build state and decide where to resume. For simple cases (resume from first failed sprint), this is expensive overkill. A --continue --heuristic flag would use deterministic rules instead.
items.fix: Add --heuristic flag to the continue flow in internal/cli/run.go. Add HeuristicAnalyze() function in internal/continuerun/analyzer.go. Rules: find first sprint without a completion marker, resume from there. Skip LLM call entirely when --heuristic is set. Update docs/commands.md. Add tests.

items.title: Targeted healing (fix specific check categories)
items.description: Currently the heal loop sends ALL failed checks to the agent every iteration. When a sprint has 10 failed checks of different types, the agent tries to fix everything at once and often fails. Targeted healing would prioritize by severity and fix one category per iteration.
items.fix: Group failed checks by type in internal/heal/heal.go. Sort groups by severity (file existence > file contents > command output). In each heal iteration, include only the highest-priority unfixed group. Track which groups are resolved across iterations. Fall back to all-at-once if targeted approach stalls (2 iterations with no progress). Update docs/self-healing.md. Add tests.

items.title: Audit finding export in SARIF format
items.description: Sprint and build audit findings are currently only in markdown. Exporting to SARIF (Static Analysis Results Interchange Format) would allow findings to appear as GitHub code annotations, VS Code problems, and in security dashboards.
items.fix: Create internal/audit/sarif.go with SARIF 2.1.0 schema types. Convert audit findings to SARIF Result objects with location, severity, message. Write build-audit.sarif alongside build-audit.md. Add --sarif flag to control output. Add build-audit.sarif to .gitignore. Update docs/build-audit.md. Add tests.

items.title: report.Write() is not atomic — partial writes left on interrupt
items.description: report.Write() calls os.WriteFile(path, data, 0o644) directly on the final destination path (report.go:60). If the process is killed (SIGKILL, OOM, power loss) mid-write, a truncated build-report.json remains on disk. Subsequent tooling that reads build-report.json for programmatic analysis will fail to parse the truncated file. The original C2 feature plan explicitly required a temp-file-then-rename pattern for atomicity.
items.fix: Replace the os.WriteFile call with a write-to-temp-then-rename pattern: create a temp file in the same directory as path (so the rename is on the same filesystem), write to it, sync, close, then os.Rename to path. This guarantees that build-report.json is either absent or fully written. Example: use os.CreateTemp(filepath.Dir(path), ".build-report-*.json"), write, close, then os.Rename(tmpFile.Name(), path).

items.title: No timeout test for CheckFileContains check type
items.description: The existing runner_test.go covers timeout behaviour for CheckCmd via TestRunnerCommandTimeout, but there is no corresponding test for CheckFileContains. Once the bug fix (CheckFileContains no per-check timeout) is applied, a test should verify that CheckFileContains is cancelled when the parent context expires rather than blocking indefinitely. Without this test, a regression would be invisible.
items.fix: Add TestRunnerCheckFileContainsTimeout in internal/verify/runner_test.go. The test should: (1) create a temp file in t.TempDir(); (2) build a CheckFileContains check against it with a pattern that would normally match; (3) supply a context with a very short deadline (e.g., 1 nanosecond, already expired); (4) call runCheck and assert that result.Passed is false, confirming that the check was cancelled rather than hanging. Call t.Parallel() as the first line. Mirror the structure of TestRunnerCommandTimeout.

items.title: BuildReport struct missing build audit findings field
items.description: The BuildReport struct (report.go:14-20) includes per-sprint verification results and token usage but has no field for build audit findings. In run.go the buildAuditResult variable is populated (and used for the SARIF export at line 948), but it is never added to buildReport before the JSON report is written (lines 933-944). Users relying on --json-report for programmatic build analysis cannot access audit findings from the JSON output, which defeats part of the purpose of structured reporting.
items.fix: Add an AuditSummary type to internal/report/report.go with fields: TotalFindings int, UnresolvedFindings int, FindingsByCategory map[string]int. Add an Audit *AuditSummary field to BuildReport. In internal/cli/run.go, before calling report.Write(), populate buildReport.Audit by converting the buildAuditResult (if non-nil) into an AuditSummary. Update docs/commands.md to mention the audit summary field in the --json-report description. Add tests for the new struct serialization.

items.title: --json-report logs relative path even when --project-dir is set
items.description: run.go line 943 logs `"  BUILD REPORT: written to build-report.json"` — a hardcoded relative path string. The actual file is written to reportPath = filepath.Join(projectPath, "build-report.json") (line 939), which may be an absolute path on a different directory when --project-dir is set. Users running fry from a directory other than the project directory will see the relative path in the log and be unable to locate the file without manual calculation.
items.fix: Replace the hardcoded string on line 943 with the absolute path already computed in reportPath: change `frlog.Log("  BUILD REPORT: written to build-report.json")` to `frlog.Log("  BUILD REPORT: written to %s", reportPath)`. This is a one-line change with no behavioural side effects.

items.title: docs/verification.md Safety Limits section omits @check_test and @check_file_contains from timeout list
items.description: docs/verification.md line 143 reads: "Per-check timeout: Command-based checks (@check_cmd and @check_cmd_output) are killed after 120 seconds to prevent hanging builds." This is inaccurate in two ways: (1) @check_test also has the 120-second timeout (runner.go:94-95) but is not listed; (2) @check_file_contains does NOT have the timeout (runner.go:51-54 uses the parent context directly), which is a bug but not documented. Users authoring verification files cannot know that @check_test is protected by a timeout or that @check_file_contains can hang indefinitely.
items.fix: Update docs/verification.md line 143 to list all four check types and their current timeout status. After the CheckFileContains timeout bug fix is applied, the corrected text should read: "Per-check timeout: All command-based checks (@check_cmd, @check_cmd_output, @check_test, and @check_file_contains) are killed after 120 seconds to prevent hanging builds." If documenting before the bug fix, note that @check_file_contains currently uses the parent context deadline instead.

items.title: README.LLM.md Key Constants table missing PlanningOutputDir entry
items.description: README.LLM.md line 313 lists WritingOutputDir = "output" in the Key Constants table but does not mention the parallel constant PlanningOutputDir = "output" defined immediately above it in internal/config/config.go. Both constants are used in internal/prepare/prepare.go (lines 98 and 103 respectively) and have the same value. The omission could cause future agents to miss PlanningOutputDir when searching the constants table for planning-mode output path configuration.
items.fix: Add a row for PlanningOutputDir to the Key Constants table in README.LLM.md immediately before or after the WritingOutputDir row: | `PlanningOutputDir` | `output` | Output directory for planning-mode deliverables |

items.title: git/git.go subprocess calls lack context — builds can hang indefinitely
items.description: All git subprocess calls in internal/git/git.go use the package-level `execCommand = exec.Command` variable (line 14), which does not accept a context. Functions InitGit, GitCheckpoint, CommitPartialWork, GitDiffForAudit, and the helpers they call (runBash, runGit, gitConfigValue, hasHead, listUntrackedPaths) cannot be cancelled or timed out. If git blocks — e.g., waiting on a pre-commit hook that prompts for input, a credential prompt for a remote, or stale network socket — the entire build hangs indefinitely with no recourse other than SIGKILL. By contrast, gitDiffStat in sprint/runner.go correctly uses exec.CommandContext. The inconsistency exists because git.go predates the context threading in the rest of the codebase.
items.fix: Thread a context.Context through InitGit, GitCheckpoint, CommitPartialWork, and GitDiffForAudit. Update runBash, runGit, gitConfigValue, hasHead, and listUntrackedPaths to accept ctx and use exec.CommandContext(ctx, ...). The package-level execCommand variable (used for test injection) should be changed to a context-aware variant, or the approach replaced with interface injection. All callers in internal/cli/run.go already hold a context. Add a timeout (e.g., 60s) for git init and git config calls specifically, since those are one-off operations.

items.title: sprint/compactor.go has no unit tests
items.description: The compactor.go file contains two functions — mechanicalCompaction and CompactSprintProgress — with no unit tests. mechanicalCompaction implements a backwards-scan to find the last '## Iteration' or '--- Heal attempt' line in a sprint progress file, then returns everything from that point forward. This determines what context is preserved across sprint boundaries and fed to the agent in subsequent sprints. A bug (e.g., off-by-one, wrong prefix match) would silently cause context loss across all sprints without any indication. No compactor_test.go file exists in internal/sprint/; only prompt_test.go and runner_test.go are present.
items.fix:
Create internal/sprint/compactor_test.go with table-driven tests for mechanicalCompaction: (1) progress file with no iteration/heal entries — returns full trimmed content; (2) file with one Iteration entry — returns from that entry onward; (3) multiple Iteration and Heal entries — returns from the LAST one; (4) empty string input; (5) last entry is a Heal attempt. Also test CompactSprintProgress in non-agent mode (useAgent=false) to verify the returned string format matches '## Sprint N: Name — Status

<compacted>
'. Call t.Parallel() on every test function.

items.title: Add `fry verify` subcommand to run checks without iteration loop
items.description: There is no way to run verification.md checks against the current project state without invoking a full sprint iteration loop. Users debugging a failed build must use --resume (which also triggers healing) or manually inspect files. A fry verify [sprint] subcommand would: (1) parse the epic file, (2) load verification.md, (3) run checks for the specified sprint (or all sprints if no sprint is given), and (4) print pass/fail results. Exit code 0 if all pass, 1 if any fail. This is useful for development workflows and lightweight CI integration.
items.fix: Add internal/cli/verify.go with a verifyCmd cobra.Command. Accept an optional positional sprint number; default to all sprint checks. Use existing verify.ParseVerification and verify.RunChecks. Print results in tabular format showing check type, description, and pass/fail. Register in root.go. Update docs/commands.md and README.md. Add integration tests in internal/cli/integration_test.go.

items.title: --show-tokens silently displays 0/0 for Ollama engine
items.description: ParseTokens("ollama", output) falls through to the default branch in tokens.go:93, which calls ParseClaudeTokens. Ollama's CLI output does not contain Claude-format token stats (input_tokens / output_tokens lines), so ParseClaudeTokens returns TokenUsage{0,0,0}. When a user runs fry with the Ollama engine and --show-tokens, the token column shows '0/0 tokens' for every sprint — which looks like a metrics bug rather than an expected limitation. The existing test in tokens_test.go:118 notes that 'unknown engines fall through to Claude parser' without mentioning Ollama specifically.
items.fix: Add an explicit 'ollama' case in ParseTokens returning TokenUsage{} with a comment: 'Ollama CLI does not report token usage; counts are always zero.' In the token display output in internal/cli/run.go, suppress the token column or display 'N/A' when the engine is Ollama and the usage is zero. Update docs/commands.md --show-tokens description to note this limitation. Add a test case in tokens_test.go: TestParseTokensOllamaReturnsZero that asserts ParseTokens("ollama", anyOutput) always returns TokenUsage{}.

items.title: runAgentWithDualLogs is duplicated in sprint and heal packages
items.description: sprint/runner.go and heal/heal.go each define a private runAgentWithDualLogs function with nearly identical logic: write agent output to a per-iteration log file and append it to an accumulative sprint log, including a Sync call. The two differ only in their parameter signatures (RunConfig vs HealOpts, plus model handling). Any fix applied to one must be manually mirrored in the other. One existing divergence: sprint's version resolves the model internally via cfg.Engine.Name(), while heal's takes the resolved model as a string parameter.
items.fix: Extract a shared runAgentWithDualLogs helper. Define a minimal DualLogOpts struct in a suitable package (e.g., internal/agentrun/ or alongside engine/): {Engine engine.Engine, Model string, ExtraFlags []string, WorkDir string, Verbose bool}. The function signature becomes runAgentWithDualLogs(ctx, prompt, iterPath, sprintLogPath string, opts DualLogOpts) (string, error). Update sprint/runner.go and heal/heal.go to call the shared function. Add a unit test for the shared function. This also removes a barrier to adding dual logging to audit and review session types.

items.title: README.LLM.md test file count is stale — says 27, actual is 37
items.description: README.LLM.md line 371 reads '**27 test files** covering all packages.' The actual count (from globbing internal/**/*_test.go) is 37 test files. New test files added since the count was written include: audit/sarif_test.go, report/report_test.go, sprint/runner_test.go, continuerun/analyzer_test.go, continuerun/collector_helpers_test.go, continuerun/report_test.go, engine/ollama_test.go, git/strategy_test.go, heal/heal_test.go, and triage/confirm_test.go. AI agents use this file as their primary orientation and may underestimate test coverage or draw incorrect conclusions from the stale count.
items.fix: Update the count in README.LLM.md from '27 test files' to '37 test files'. Consider replacing the hardcoded number with a description like 'Test files in every package (currently 37)' to make future drift more obvious. This is a one-line change.
